title: Determining Why the Author Wrote This

steps.instruction: Read: 'Brush your teeth at least twice a day. Use a soft toothbrush and fluoride toothpaste. Replace your toothbrush every three months.'
steps.work: Brush your teeth at least twice a day. Use a soft toothbrush and fluoride toothpaste. Replace your toothbrush every three months.
steps.explanation: Let's figure out WHY the author wrote this.

steps.subgoalLabel: Think about the three main purposes
steps.instruction: Authors write to: (1) inform — teach facts, (2) persuade — change your mind, (3) entertain — tell a fun story. Which one fits?
steps.work: This passage gives instructions about brushing teeth. It teaches how to take care of your teeth.
steps.explanation: The text is giving factual instructions, not a funny story and not an opinion.

steps.work: The author wrote this to inform readers about how to brush their teeth correctly.
steps.explanation: The purpose is to inform. Clues: facts, instructions, no jokes or opinions. Informational text teaches you something.

steps.instruction: Read: 'Our school should have longer recess. Kids need time to run and play. Studies show that exercise helps students focus better in class. Please tell the principal you want more recess!'
steps.work: Our school should have longer recess. Kids need time to run and play. Studies show that exercise helps students focus better in class. Please tell the principal you want more recess!
steps.explanation: Is this informing us or trying to change our mind?

steps.subgoalLabel: Look for opinion words and calls to action
steps.instruction: Does the author give an opinion? Does the author ask you to DO something?
steps.work: Opinion: 'should have longer recess.' Call to action: 'Please tell the principal.'
steps.explanation: 'Should' signals an opinion. 'Please tell the principal' asks the reader to take action.

steps.work: The author wrote this to persuade readers that recess should be longer.
steps.explanation: The purpose is to persuade. Even though there are facts (exercise helps focus), the author's main goal is to convince you to agree with an opinion. Persuasive writing uses opinions, reasons, and calls to action.
